items.title: 360ONE delivered solid FY26 results with 21.5% revenue growth and 19.8% PAT growth, meeting revenue guidance at the high end while slightly missing PA...
items.description_html:
360ONE delivered solid FY26 results with 21.5% revenue growth and 19.8% PAT growth, meeting revenue guidance at the high end while slightly missing PAT guidance. The company showed strong execution on AUM growth (26.4% vs 22-24% target) and Net Flows (14.2% vs 10-12% target), with TBR recovering in Q4.

However, margin pressure persists with EBITDA margin contracting 214 bps, and Asset Management segment showing significant margin compression. A contingent tax liability of ₹336 Cr poses a notable risk. The stock is trading in-line with guidance with no major surprises.

Positive Factors

Total Income grew 21.5% YoY to ₹4,477.3 Cr for FY26

Q4 FY26 Revenue from Operations grew 27.3% YoY to ₹1,115.5 Cr

Net gain on fair value changes surged 155.5% YoY in Q4 to ₹239.2 Cr

PAT grew 19.8% YoY to ₹1,216.2 Cr for FY26

Loan book expanded 32.3% YoY to ₹11,126 Cr

Goodwill increased 339.9% YoY to ₹2,498 Cr reflecting acquisitions

Net Flows of 14.2% exceeded management target of 10-12%

ARR AUM grew 26.4% YoY meeting 22-24% guidance

EBITDA grew 25.0% YoY to ₹2,706.7 Cr

TBR recovered to ₹230 Cr in Q4, up 53.7% YoY

Risk Factors

PAT growth of 20.7% was slightly below 22-24% guidance target

EBITDA margin contracted 214 bps YoY to 62.1%

Asset Management PBT margin contracted 658 bps to 52.7%

Q4 PBT margin for Asset Management fell 894 bps YoY to 56.9%

Contingent tax liability of ₹336.14 Cr represents 3.4% of net worth

Operating cash flow deeply negative at ₹2,921 Cr due to loan disbursements

items.title: TARIL missed its FY26 revenue guidance (₹2,395.5 Cr vs ₹2,600 Cr target) and EBITDA margin guidance (15.44% vs 16-17% target), with working capital ...
items.description_html:
TARIL missed its FY26 revenue guidance (₹2,395.5 Cr vs ₹2,600 Cr target) and EBITDA margin guidance (15.44% vs 16-17% target), with working capital deteriorating to 207 days against a 120-day target.

The company generated negative operating cash flow of ₹105.38 Cr and is funding operations through borrowings, indicating significant execution challenges despite healthy revenue growth of 24.2% YoY.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 24.2% YoY to ₹2,508.80 Cr (FY26)

PAT grew 25.8% YoY to ₹272.17 Cr (FY26)

EBITDA margin improved 20 bps QoQ to 15.1% in Q4

Capital work-in-progress doubled YoY to ₹138.31 Cr indicating capacity expansion

Subsidiary contribution to PBT is disproportionately high at 16.9% despite only 4.5% of revenue

FY26 capex of ₹109.44 Cr is on track for the multi-period ₹550 Cr plan

Risk Factors

Missed revenue guidance: ₹2,395.5 Cr actual vs ₹2,600 Cr target (7.9% shortfall)

Missed EBITDA margin guidance: 15.44% actual vs 16-17% target range

Working capital days at 206.8 vs target of 120 days - severe blockage

Net Cash from Operating Activities turned negative at ₹(105.38) Cr

Total debt increased 61.5% YoY to ₹456.61 Cr, company borrowing to fund operations

Cost of Materials Consumed grew 24.4% YoY vs 15.7% revenue growth - margin pressure

items.description_html:
The world’s top condom maker says it plans to raise prices by 20%-30% and possibly further due to the impact of the US-Iran war in the petrochemical industry.

Malaysia's Karex Bhd told Reuters is also seeing a surge in condom demand as rising freight costs and shipping delays have left many of its customers with lower stockpiles than usual.

Karex produces over 5 billion condoms annually and is a supplier to leading brands like Durex.

items.title: Persistent delivered a significant margin beat with FY26 Adjusted EBIT margin of 16.36% exceeding guidance of 15.7% by 66 bps. Revenue grew 23.5% YoY ...
items.description_html:
Persistent delivered a significant margin beat with FY26 Adjusted EBIT margin of 16.36% exceeding guidance of 15.7% by 66 bps. Revenue grew 23.5% YoY to ₹147,484.49 million keeping the company on track for $2B target by FY27.

Free cash flow surged 67.9% YoY with superior cash conversion of 0.94x CFO to PAT ratio, while BFSI segment led growth at 35.1% YoY and Software/Hi-Tech achieved remarkable 586 bps margin expansion.

Positive Factors

FY26 Adjusted EBIT margin of 16.36% beat guidance of 15.7% by 66 bps

Revenue grew 25.1% YoY in Q4 to ₹40,559.37 million, 23.5% for full year

PAT grew 33.7% YoY in Q4 to ₹5,292.60 million, 33.2% for full year

Free cash flow increased 67.9% YoY to ₹15,485.49 million

BFSI segment revenue grew 33.4% YoY in Q4, fastest growth driver

Software/Hi-Tech segment margin expanded 586 bps for full year FY26

Healthcare segment rebounded to 22.7% YoY growth in Q4

Total dividend for FY26 at ₹40 per share

Risk Factors

Subcontracting costs grew 32.7% YoY, faster than revenue growth of 25.1%

Other expenses rose 40.2% YoY, outpacing revenue growth

Depreciation grew 37.2% YoY to ₹1,085.39 million, faster than revenue

Healthcare segment margin declined 112 bps YoY in Q4 to 37.5%

BFSI segment margin declined 104 bps YoY for full year to 34.7%

items.title: MAHEPC FY26 showed strong full-year performance with revenue up 14.5% and PAT up 76%, meeting the operating margin target of 6-8%. However, Q4 was wea...
items.description_html:
MAHEPC FY26 showed strong full-year performance with revenue up 14.5% and PAT up 76%, meeting the operating margin target of 6-8%. However, Q4 was weak with PAT down 23% YoY and severe working capital stress emerged - operating cash flow was -₹16.28 Cr, receivables grew 40%, and debt increased 78%.

The company achieved its profitability targets but cash generation deteriorated significantly, creating near-term liquidity concerns despite solid top-line and earnings growth.

Positive Factors

FY26 revenue grew 14.5% YoY to ₹312.09 Cr

FY26 PAT grew 76.0% YoY to ₹12.69 Cr

FY26 EBITDA grew 43.6% YoY to ₹20.67 Cr

FY26 EBITDA margin expanded 134 bps to 6.6%

Operating margin of 6.5% met management's 6-8% target

Non-subsidy business reached 37.8% of revenue in H1 FY26

ROE improved to 7.0% from 4.27% in FY25

Risk Factors

Q4 PAT declined 23.4% YoY despite 11.6% revenue growth - significant margin pressure

Operating cash flow severely negative at -₹16.28 Cr despite ₹12.69 Cr PAT

Trade receivables ballooned 40% YoY to ₹247 Cr, debtor days increased to 289

Total debt increased 78.4% YoY to ₹45.31 Cr

Cash nearly wiped out at ₹0.03 Cr vs ₹1.69 Cr prior year

Q4 EBITDA margin contracted 356 bps YoY to 6.6%

Working capital change impact on CFO was -₹42.09 Cr

items.title: Nestlé India delivered exceptional Q4 FY26 results with 22.6% YoY revenue growth to ₹6,747.8 Cr and EBITDA margin expansion of 110 bps to 26.3%, des...
items.description_html:
Nestlé India delivered exceptional Q4 FY26 results with 22.6% YoY revenue growth to ₹6,747.8 Cr and EBITDA margin expansion of 110 bps to 26.3%, despite commodity cost pressures.

The company achieved its strongest quarterly growth in nearly a decade driven by double-digit volume growth across all product categories. Exceptional cash generation with 72% YoY CFO growth to ₹5,047.6 Cr and virtually debt-free balance sheet provide strong financial flexibility for continued investment in growth.

Positive Factors

Q4 revenue grew 22.6% YoY to ₹6,747.8 Cr, fastest growth in nearly a decade

EBITDA margin expanded 110 bps YoY to 26.3% despite commodity cost pressures

PAT grew 27.2% YoY to ₹1,110.9 Cr in Q4

CFO surged 72% YoY to ₹5,047.6 Cr with FCF of ₹4,217.9 Cr

Domestic sales grew 23.1% YoY, Export sales grew 31.0% YoY

All four product groups delivered positive volume-led growth

Advertising spend increased over 50% YoY to drive brand growth

Virtually debt-free with total borrowings at ₹244.4 million

Risk Factors

Cost of materials as % of sales increased from 43.6% to 44.8% in FY26 due to commodity price pressures

Other expenses grew 24.4% YoY driven by higher advertising spend

Full year normalized PAT growth moderate at 10.9% vs Q4's 30.2%

Raw material availability and price volatility remain ongoing risks

items.title: RT Mangalam Maloo: Nestle Q4FY26: Solid Beat Stock at Record High Stellar Set Revenue +22.6% at 6748 Cr vs 5504 Cr (Poll: 6250 Cr) EBITDA +27.6% at 17...
items.description_html:
RT Mangalam Maloo
Nestle Q4FY26: Solid Beat
Stock at Record High

Stellar Set
Revenue +22.6% at 6748 Cr vs 5504 Cr (Poll: 6250 Cr)
EBITDA +27.6% at 1771.5 Cr vs 1389 Cr (Poll: 1530 Cr)
Margins at 26.3% vs 25.2%
Gross Margins at 55.7% vs 56.2%
PAT +25.5% at 1111 Cr vs 885.4 Cr (Poll: 998 Cr)

Key Internals
- Double Digit Volume growth across categories
- Domestic sales for the quarter increased by 23.4%
- Confectionery product group grew at a high double-digit pace in both value and volume
- Prepared Dishes and Cooking Aids product posted strong volume-driven growth
- Powdered and Liquid Beverages product group achieved another year of high double-digit growth
- Pet food business reported high double-digit growth
- Milk Products and Nutrition product group showed resilience, delivering steady growth
-Progressed on structural cost-efficiency agenda; delivered our highest-ever operational cost savings
- All business channels recorded strong double-digit growth

Commodity Outlook
- Coffee prices continue to trend lower
- Cocoa prices remain subdued
- Edible oil prices are firm and have moved higher in line with global crude oil prices
- Wheat has been affected by unseasonal rains in April
- Milk prices have firmed and are expected to remain elevated

Nestle
Stock At Record High
Trades at 61X FY27e

@CNBCTV18Live #FMCGisLife @NestleIndia #StockMarket

items.title: Strong results from PNB Housing - more importantly Commentary is positive🟩 This is creating positive rub off across the Housing finance sector (mai...
items.description_html:
Strong results from PNB Housing - more importantly Commentary is positive🟩

This is creating positive rub off across the Housing finance sector (mainly affordable housing finance)

- Loan book expected to cross 1 Lakh Cr in FY27 - overall growth guidance 18-20%

- Retail loan book projected to grow 18-20% - growth to be driven mainly by affordable & emerging segments

- Affordable segment expected to grow fastest - management guiding ~50% growth in disbursements

- Affordable + emerging mix currently at 40% - expected to move towards ~50% over next 2 to 3 years supporting yield improvement & margin expansion

- Management indicated yields have likely bottomed out - expected to improve with higher mix of emerging, affordable and corporate segments

- ROA guidance at 2.4-2.5% for FY27 supported by mix improvement & recoveries

- Credit cost expected to remain negative again in FY27 (~15 to 30 bps benefit) due to recovery from legacy pool

- GNPA improved below 1% (0.93%) - asset quality remains healthy

Management clearly shifting portfolio mix toward affordable & emerging segments due to higher growth visibility

items.title: As a country gets richer and simultaneously ages, deaths from communicable diseases and conditions related to childbirth become more preventable and l...
items.description_html:
As a country gets richer and simultaneously ages, deaths from communicable diseases and conditions related to childbirth become more preventable and less common. Consequently, the relative share of deaths from non-communicable diseases such as heart disease and cancer begins to rise.

India is in the midst of this epidemiological transition. Non-communicable diseases account for over half of all deaths in India as of 2022, the latest year for which this data is available.

Among non-communicable diseases, heart disease is the largest contributor to Indian mortality and is now responsible for one in three deaths. From middle age onwards as the relative risk from infectious diseases declines, age and factors including obesity and hypertension put people at greater risk.

Cancer kills over 6% of Indians every year, with a higher contribution to mortality among those in their late 40s and early 50s in particular. 15% of deaths of women aged 30-54 are from cancer.

What are the top causes of death from communicable diseases in India? Read @Rukmini's piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/indias-disease-transition/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Diseases #Health #PublicHealth #India #DataForIndia

items.description_html:
AI adoption is surging among US employees:

50% of employed American adults used AI in their role at least a few times in Q1 2026, up from 46% in Q4 2025, according to a Gallup survey.

This percentage has more than DOUBLED over the last 3 years.

Furthermore, 13% of surveyed employees used AI daily last quarter, while 28% used it at least a few times a week.

Meanwhile, 27% of employees in AI-adopting organizations reported their workplace has changed in disruptive ways over the last year.

AI usage among the American workforce is accelerating.

items.title: Massive buybacks are coming to the US market: Buyback authorizations across the Russell 3000 surged +36% YoY, to a record $428 billion so far in 2026....
items.description_html:
Massive buybacks are coming to the US market:

Buyback authorizations across the Russell 3000 surged +36% YoY, to a record $428 billion so far in 2026.

Authorizations are now +176% above the same period in 2020.

At a historical execution rate of ~90%, US corporates are now on track to repurchase a record ~$1 trillion in shares this year.

In 2 weeks, the corporate buyback window will reopen following the earnings blackout period.

This will bring an additional wave of demand for stocks at a time when the market is already experiencing a historic run.

US corporations are set to be major buyers in the equity market.

items.title: US electricity bills are surging: Average US residential electricity prices rose +5% YoY, to a record 17.3 cents per kilowatt-hour in 2025. Since Janu...
items.description_html:
US electricity bills are surging:

Average US residential electricity prices rose +5% YoY, to a record 17.3 cents per kilowatt-hour in 2025.

Since January 2020, electricity prices have risen +30% or more in at least 11 US states, with the steepest increases concentrated in the Northeast and Midwest.

In parts of eastern Pennsylvania, electricity prices have surged +200% over this period, adding ~$23 per month to the average household bill this year alone.

Data centers are expected to add at least $23 billion to customer bills on PJM Interconnection, the nation's largest power grid, over the 3 years ending May 2028.

This comes as US summer power demand is projected to rise +224 gigawatts over the next decade, enough to power an additional 180 million homes, driven primarily by data center expansion, electric vehicles, and heat pumps.

Rising electricity bills are a major economic issue.

items.description_html:
The world is heading into a historic debt crisis:

Global government debt is projected to surge to 102% of GDP by 2031, a level previously seen only in the aftermath of World War II, according to the IMF.

World government debt already stands at ~94% of GDP.

Debt-to-GDP has risen +16 percentage points since 2015, with the US and China responsible for the majority of the increase.

This comes as the US is running a government deficit of 7-8% of GDP, with debt projected to rise to 142% of GDP by 2031.

At the same time, China’s Debt-to-GDP is expected to increase to 127% as the country’s deficit has widened to nearly 8% of GDP.

Global interest payments are expected to rise to ~5% of GDP by 2031 from the current ~3%, as governments refinance maturing debt at today's higher borrowing costs.

The global economy is being increasingly fueled by debt.

items.title: BREAKING: Chinese exports rose +3% YoY in March, the smallest increase since October 2025. This marks a sharp slowdown from the +40% surge in February...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: Chinese exports rose +3% YoY in March, the smallest increase since October 2025.

This marks a sharp slowdown from the +40% surge in February.

Chinese imports surged +28% YoY last month, the highest since late 2021, driven by a +50% jump in integrated circuit purchases as AI-related demand accelerated.

Furthermore, nearly 40% of total import growth came from high-tech products.

As a result, China's trade surplus narrowed to +$51 billion, the smallest in over a year.

Meanwhile, exports to the US fell -26% YoY in March, resuming the decline after a brief recovery in February.

AI and geopolitics are reshaping Chinese trade.

items.title: The housing market is running out of buyers: There were an estimated 46.3% more home sellers than buyers in February 2026, the biggest difference sinc...
items.description_html:
The housing market is running out of buyers:

There were an estimated 46.3% more home sellers than buyers in February 2026, the biggest difference since Redfin data began in 2013.

This gap has widened sharply from 29.8% a year ago, marking the strongest market for buyers in over a decade.

By comparison, there were a record 36.0% more buyers than sellers in November 2022.

This comes as the number of active homebuyers fell -2.4% MoM, to ~1.36 million, the lowest since at least 2013.

At the same time, the number of sellers declined -0.4% MoM to ~1.99 million, posting a smaller decrease than homebuyers.

Further home price cuts are likely coming.

items.title: US small businesses are pulling back on investment as if we are in a crisis: In March, only 16% of small businesses said that they plan on investing i...
items.description_html:
US small businesses are pulling back on investment as if we are in a crisis:

In March, only 16% of small businesses said that they plan on investing in CapEx over the next 6 months, the lowest reading since November 2009.

This percentage has declined -12 points since November 2024.

By comparison, the pre-2008 Financial Crisis and the 2020 pandemic levels were 30-35%.

Furthermore, the proportion of firms investing in CapEx over the last 6 months has declined -9 points since January, and remains below the long-term historical average.

This comes as policy uncertainty and elevated energy costs are weighing on small businesses.

All while they cite taxes, quality of labor, and inflation as their most important problems.

Small businesses are struggling in the US.

items.title: For those who are interested in Japan's recent economic history, and what that may tell us about other economies that copied its growth model, it is w...
items.description_html:
For those who are interested in Japan's recent economic history, and what that may tell us about other economies that copied its growth model, it is worth noting that in Japan, the term “excess competition” (過当競争, katō kyōsō) emerged, mostly in the 1960s, to express what was seen at the time as an urgent problem.

Japanese officials used it to mean competition so intense that it wiped out profitability, discouraged private investment, and created instability in key industries. It also led to financial fragility because of the associated dependence of Japanese companies on unlimited access to easy credit to stay alive.

items.title: Mr Ramesh Damani - "The way you do investing is - you keep a circle of competence and do your homework, don’t get complacent and lazy, which we tend ...
items.description_html:
Mr Ramesh Damani -

"The way you do investing is - you keep a circle of competence and do your homework, don’t get complacent and lazy, which we tend to be after some time.

If you are generally a smart person, then there will be tons and tons of opportunity. Here is the thing that RK Damani told me. I used to ask him in the 1990s, after he had made tons of money and I hadn’t . I said “you people are lucky that you bought Indian stocks in the 1980s. Aap logon ka paisa bangaya. Mein abhi aaya hoon. Mera Nahi Banega” (“You people have made money. I have just come. I won’t make money.”)

He said, “Ramesh, I am telling you this today. The opportunity in the next 20 years will be far greater than those in the past 20 years. Trust me on that.” He has been proven absolutely correct.

In December 2016, there was a conference in Kolkata. Someone asked him the same question and he answered the same - “The best time to invest in India is today. The opportunity of the last 30 years will be dwarfed by the opportunity of the next 30 years.” I also truly believe that.

From the Book Masterclass with Super Investors

items.title: E2E delivered a strong Q4 FY26 with revenue growth of 185.7% YoY and EBITDA margin expansion to 60.7%, demonstrating clear operating leverage from GPU...
items.description_html:
E2E delivered a strong Q4 FY26 with revenue growth of 185.7% YoY and EBITDA margin expansion to 60.7%, demonstrating clear operating leverage from GPU infrastructure investments. The company swung from quarterly loss to profit and exceeded its Gross Block guidance by 42.6%.

While full-year profitability missed guidance due to front-loaded depreciation, the Q4 inflection and strong cash flow generation indicate the business has reached a scalability threshold with significant competitive advantages in the AI cloud infrastructure market.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 185.7% YoY to ₹9,564.27 Lakhs in Q4

EBITDA surged 335.5% YoY to ₹5,810.22 Lakhs with 60.7% margin

PBT swung from loss in Q3 to profit of ₹855.82 Lakhs in Q4

Gross Block of ₹1,569 Cr exceeded guidance of ₹1,100 Cr by 42.6%

Operating cash flow at ₹12,205.68 Lakhs with 0.97x EBITDA conversion

Q4 revenue grew 36.6% QoQ showing continued momentum

Trade receivables grew 75.6% YoY indicating strong sales execution

Risk Factors

Full-year FY26 net loss of ₹1,556.59 Lakhs due to heavy depreciation

Depreciation increased 181.7% YoY to ₹16,922.69 Lakhs

EBITDA margin of 51.4% for FY26 missed guidance of 65-75%

Cash and cash equivalents decreased 30.7% YoY to ₹32,157.10 Lakhs

Other income declined 78.4% YoY impacting total income

Working capital needs may increase with rapid revenue growth

items.title: PNB Gilts reported a severe quarterly collapse with PBT down 85.7% YoY due to a massive ₹7,253 Lacs trading loss that entirely negated strong NII gro...
items.description_html:
PNB Gilts reported a severe quarterly collapse with PBT down 85.7% YoY due to a massive ₹7,253 Lacs trading loss that entirely negated strong NII growth of 113.2% YoY.

While the core interest-earning business showed robust performance with NII margin expanding 1118 bps to 25.1%, the trading book volatility confirms high interest-rate sensitivity and revenue concentration risks. The dividend increase to ₹2/share and strong capital adequacy (52.68%) provide some balance sheet comfort, but execution quality was poor with significant profit decline.

Positive Factors

NII grew 113.2% YoY to ₹10,587.18 Lacs in Q4

NII Margin expanded 1118 bps YoY to 25.1%

Total Income grew 1.4% YoY to ₹1,69,906 Lacs for FY26

Dividend increased to ₹2 per share from ₹1 in FY25

Capital Adequacy Ratio at 52.68% vs RBI requirement of 15%

Balance sheet expanded 9.5% YoY to ₹27,06,044 Lacs

Risk Factors

PAT collapsed 82.7% YoY to ₹1,299.17 Lacs in Q4

PBT fell 85.7% YoY to ₹1,416.22 Lacs in Q4

Massive trading loss of ₹7,252.77 Lacs in Q4 vs gain of ₹5,891.85 Lacs in Q4 FY25

Total expenses grew 28.3% YoY, outpacing 1.3% income growth

RoNOF declined to 11.30% from 16.46% in FY25

Fee income declined 27.8% YoY to ₹242.81 Lacs indicating diversification challenges

items.title: Navkar Corporation delivered a strong turnaround in FY26 with EBITDA of ₹118.16 crore exceeding management guidance of ₹100 crore by 18.16%. The com...
items.description_html:
Navkar Corporation delivered a strong turnaround in FY26 with EBITDA of ₹118.16 crore exceeding management guidance of ₹100 crore by 18.16%. The company transformed from a net loss of ₹45.30 crore to net profit of ₹30.15 crore, with EBITDA margin expanding by 1548 bps to 17.2%.

While working capital stress (58% receivables growth) and low RoCE (~3%) remain concerns, the execution on guidance and strong cash generation indicate improving operational efficiency.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 41.1% YoY to ₹687.46 crore

EBITDA grew 1319.9% YoY to ₹118.16 crore

EBITDA margin expanded 1548 bps YoY to 17.2%

PAT turned positive at ₹30.15 crore vs loss of ₹45.30
crore in FY25

EPS turned positive at ₹2.00 vs -₹3.01 in FY25

Beat EBITDA guidance of ₹100 crore by 18.16%

Free cash flow positive at ₹21.61 crore

Finance costs declined 23.3% YoY

Risk Factors

Trade receivables grew 58% YoY outpacing revenue growth of 41.1%

Net debt to EBITDA at 1.26x indicating moderate leverage

RoCE at ~3% remains low despite improvement

Short-term borrowings increased 49.9% YoY

Effective tax rate high at 38.1%

items.title: Bank of Maharashtra delivered an exceptional Q4 FY26 with consolidated PAT growing 36.1% YoY to ₹204.49 Crore, beating its own guidance across all ke...
items.description_html:
Bank of Maharashtra delivered an exceptional Q4 FY26 with consolidated PAT growing 36.1% YoY to ₹204.49 Crore, beating its own guidance across all key metrics including total business growth (17.5% vs 15% target) and advances growth (22.0% vs 17% target).

The bank demonstrated strong execution with margin inflections (cost-to-income at 37.1%), capital adequacy reversal (CET1 improved 149 bps QoQ to 14.59%), and asset quality improvement (GNPA at 1.45%). Retail Banking emerged as a new growth driver with PBT up 176.8% YoY.

Positive Factors

Net Profit grew 36.1% YoY to ₹204.49 Crore (consolidated) driven by 16.9% PPOP growth and 37.3% reduction in provisions

Advances growth of 22.0% YoY significantly exceeded the 17% guidance target

Total business growth of 17.5% YoY exceeded the 15% guidance target

Cost-to-income ratio improved to 37.1% for FY26, well below the sub-40% guidance

CET1 ratio rebounded to 14.59% in Q4 from 13.10% in Q3, reversing prior declining trend

Retail Banking PBT surged 176.8% YoY to ₹93.46 Crore, becoming key profit driver

Asset quality improved with GNPA at 1.45% and Net NPA at 0.13%, provision coverage at 98.59%

Capital Adequacy Ratio (CRAR) improved to 18.36% from 17.06% QoQ, well above 16% guidance

Risk Factors

Capital Adequacy (CRAR) declined 217 bps YoY to 18.36% from 20.53% despite quarterly improvement

Other Income declined 4.3% YoY to ₹93,828 lakhs in
Q4, showing volatility in non-interest income

CET1 ratio at 14.59% remains 124 bps lower than Q4 FY25 level of 15.83%

Negative cash flow from operating activities of ₹10,988.79 Crore for FY26 due to high loan book expansion

Associate loss share of ₹3.78 Crore offset subsidiary profit contribution in consolidated PAT

Impairment loss of ₹280.59 crore on Maharashtra Gramin Bank investment booked in standalone P&L

items.title: RT CNBC-TV18: ‘India and China had the same per capita GDP in 1990. Today, China is 5X ahead.’ So what went wrong? On Young Turks Reloaded, Co-found...
items.description_html:
RT CNBC-TV18
‘India and China had the same per capita GDP in 1990. Today, China is 5X ahead.’

So what went wrong?

On Young Turks Reloaded, Co-founder of TeamLease and Co-author of Made In India, Manish Sabharwal delivers a blunt reality check -
‘The most embarrassing number about India? 35 years after 1991, 45% of our labour force still works on farms.’

But here’s the twist - Manufacturing alone won’t fix it. ‘We will never have 28%-45% of our workforce in factories.’

The real battleground? ‘We don’t have a shortage of land, labour or capital, we have a problem in how they combine.’

And it starts early:
📚 Nearly half of India’s children depend on government schools - where quality remains deeply inconsistent
🌏 In contrast, only 5% in Japan, 10% in the UK and 15% in the US go to private schools - because public education works

‘If anything should be free and high-quality, it is school education.’

The takeaway:
China didn’t win because of communism, it won because it unleashed entrepreneurship.
India’s path? Fix education. Unlock entrepreneurs.

Catch the full show - https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4q3cc9HNPyc&t=265s

@ShereenBhan

items.title: GROWW delivered exceptional Q4 FY26 with revenue up 88% YoY and PAT up 122% YoY, driven by strong operating leverage as expenses grew only 37.6% YoY. ...
items.description_html:
GROWW delivered exceptional Q4 FY26 with revenue up 88% YoY and PAT up 122% YoY, driven by strong operating leverage as expenses grew only 37.6% YoY. EBITDA margin expanded 1395 bps to 62.3%. No prior management guidance exists to benchmark against, but normalized PAT growth of 28.1% YoY confirms robust underlying performance.

The company successfully executed its quality-over-quantity strategy in F&O while scaling new businesses like MTF and subsidiaries.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 88.0% YoY to ₹15,053.67 million in Q4

PAT grew 122.1% YoY to ₹6,863.54 million in Q4

EBITDA margin expanded 1395 bps YoY to 62.3% in Q4

Normalized PAT grew 28.1% YoY removing one-time items impact

Subsidiaries contributed 34.1% of revenue and 21.8% of PAT

Total debt reduced 69.2% YoY to ₹1,459.41 million

Finance costs declined 49.8% YoY reflecting strengthened balance sheet

Risk Factors

Operating cash flow negative at ₹206.03 million for FY26 despite PBT of ₹28,214.11 million

Working capital tied up in loan book with ₹25,954.70 million deployed into loans

Employee costs rose 87.5% YoY for full year reflecting acquisition integration

D&A surged 94.6% YoY due to Finwizard intangible amortization

items.description_html:
RT Spharaka Networks
This weekend, a multi-billion dollar tech company was breached.

Not through malware. Not through a zero-day. Not through phishing.

Through an AI tool that had OAuth access to an employee's workspace.

The attacker compromised a third-party AI application, inherited its trusted permissions, pivoted into internal systems, and accessed secrets and deployment pipelines, all without writing a single exploit.

This is the new kill chain. And most enterprises are completely unprepared for it.

Every AI agent connected to your email, documents, code repositories, or cloud infrastructure is a privileged insider operating at machine speed. When that agent is compromised, the attacker doesn't need your password. They already have your trust.

The numbers should concern every security leader:

→ 490% increase in AI-related attacks year over year
→ 48% of security professionals rank agentic AI as the #1 attack vector of 2026
→ 86% of organisations have zero visibility into where their AI tools are connected
→ Over half of deployed AI agents operate without any security monitoring

This is the threat model we built Spharaka Sphere™ to defend against, autonomous, AI-native defence that monitors identity chains, detects trust anomalies, and responds at machine speed before human analysts even see an alert.

In a world where attackers use AI agents, defenders need autonomous AI agents too.

The next breach won't come through your firewall. It will come through an integration you already approved.

#CyberSecurity #AIAgents #OAuthSecurity #AttackSurface #SaaSSecurity #IdentitySecurity #SupplyChainRisk #AutonomousDefence #SpharakaNetworks #SpharakaSphere #AuraXP #AgenticAI #CISO #EnterpriseSecurity #InfoSec

https://open.substack.com/pub/spharaka/p/the-trusted-agent-how-ai-copilots?utm_campaign=post-expanded-share&utm_medium=web

items.title: Samsung Electronics is currently trading at nearly 5x fwd earnings That looks surprising despite the stock being up ~300% YoY Reason - earnings have b...
items.description_html:
Samsung Electronics is currently trading at nearly 5x fwd earnings

That looks surprising despite the stock being up ~300% YoY

Reason - earnings have been extremely strong, driven by the memory upcycle due to AI led demand for HBM & DRAM

Memory is a highly cyclical industry that goes through deep cycles

When the cycle is in an upturn, P/E looks very low because earnings are strong

When the cycle turns down, P/E starts looking high

So a low P/E here does not automatically mean cheap - it usually reflects where we are in the upcycle

Let’s see how this plays out from here

items.title: Finally some good progress on Market Coupling - Round Robin system is no more - Grid India takes Operator status to match bids as a neutral party - Gr...
items.description_html:
Finally some good progress on Market Coupling

- Round Robin system is no more
- Grid India takes Operator status to match bids as a neutral party
- Grid India has 6 months to come up with the Market coupling algorithm similar to Europe's Day Ahead Market
- Post 6 months, then after everyone's approval they have to build the systems, the infrastructure, cybersecurity protection etc etc

Looks like this should take 12 to 18months to see some value

This is assuming IEX does not put new cases on the order to stall

Now contrary to popular belief - Market Coupling like the EU is actually good

- EU trading Market boomed post market Coupling
- The industry leader EPEX Spot who was a market leader before coupling still has most of the volume
- EU has only 2 players taking all the market share with Nord Pool being 2nd

IEX
- Yes market coupling without round Robin is good, but market coupling itself will take some market share
- At best market share should dip from 99% to 75%.
- At best post market coupling they would stall revenues for 1 year
- Post market coupling the regulator will boost Power trading a lot
- Volume will rise
- News instruments like CfD coming to power exchanges

Verdict
Short term IEX will be volatile
For long term investors the game will start post Market Coupling

Today Indias largest Power exchange is at close to a billion dollars.

Disc - Invested in IEX at Lower Levels. Not a buy or sell reco

items.title: RT Nimesh Shah: Nomura on Yes Bk Neutral, TP Rs 21 4Q26: Improving fundamentals; core-ROAs still catching up Broad-based strength across growth, margi...
items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
Nomura on Yes Bk
Neutral, TP Rs 21
4Q26: Improving fundamentals; core-ROAs still catching up
Broad-based strength across growth, margins and profitability
ROA at 0.95%; SR recoveries to moderate from FY26 levels
Levers for profitability at play, but core-ROA profile build up would take time
Strategic execution of new management is a key monitorable

MS on Yes Bk
UW. TP Rs 15
Core PPOP was in line vs MSe.
PAT miss of 2% was due to Rs3.4bn one-off provisions.
Credit costs were stable, aided by recoveries.
NIM improved QoQ and was above MSe.
Balance sheet growth improved to 11% YoY (3% in F3Q)
Stay UW given valuation significantly above fundamentals.

JPM on Yes BK
UW, TP Rs 18
operating performance in
4QFY26 beat JPMe with PAT of Rs 10.7bn (+45% y/y, +12% q/q) coming 9% above JPMe, driven by
(i) cost control – opex rose by just 2% YoY, and came 2% lower vs JPMe.
(ii) CIR improved to 63%, but remains elevated vs peers and continues to weigh on RoA.
(iii) 28bps of credit costs booked during qtr were 4bps lower vs. JPMe
Yes Bank achieved an RoA of 1% for first time since reconstruction, however, supported by 31bps of contribution from SR recoveries
Believe that improving visibility on sustainability of these levels remains key, as risks from MSMEs are likely to emerge from supply chain disruptions, driving credit costs higher
Believe valuations at 1.2x P/B FY27E already price in much of b/s growth & RoA improvements.

@CNBCTV18News @_RituSingh @latha_venkatesh

items.title: RT Nimesh Shah: UBS on HDFC BK Buy, TP Rs 1175 Q4 PAT beat driven by lower provisions Loans grew c12% YoY; LDR down c400bp QoQ to 94.6% Management exp...
items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
UBS on HDFC BK
Buy, TP Rs 1175
Q4 PAT beat driven by lower provisions
Loans grew c12% YoY; LDR down c400bp QoQ to 94.6%
Management expects loan growth momentum to sustain
Expect c14-15% ROE in FY27-28E

MS on HDFC BK
OW. TP Rs 1025
PAT beat MSe by 4% as sharply lower provisions and lower operating costs offset slightly lower core revenue (2% below MSe).
Commentary was anchored on loan growth with LDR no longer a constraint.
Earnings resilience and attractive valuation make it one of key picks amid geopolitical risks.

CLSA on HDFC BK
O-P, TP Rs 1200
4QFY26 NII and PPOP were largely in line, while PBT was a 4% beat
Would characterise it as a steady performance in a slightly challenging quarter.
Loan growth of 12% YoY was largely in line
Sequential quarterly-average (QA) deposit growth picked up from the past couple of quarters.
LDR (a metric we consider irrelevant, but many investors still find important) declined to 95% from 99% in prior quarter.
Margins were up 3bp QoQ, a decent outcome in this environment when cost of wholesale deposits rose sharply post the Gulf conflict.
HDFCB’s gross slippages have declined YoY on an absolute basis for three consecutive quarters.

JPM on HDFC BK
OW, TP Rs 990
Delivered a steady 4Q26 with PAT broadly in line vs JPMe.
While NII (-4% vs JPMe) and NIMs trailed JPMe on elevated competitive intensity and an adverse product mix, they were offset by lower than expected loan loss provisions.
NII growth for HDFCB has remained volatile, decelerating to 3.2% y/y in 4Q26 vs 4.8%/6.4% in 2Q/3Q, respectively
Expect NII growth to accelerate in FY27/28 on a better-than-expected pick-up in system credit growth and funding cost benefits from a decline in the share of higher cost borrowings.
Decline in average LCR to 114% (vs 116% in 3Q, 110-120% target range) does reduce balance-sheet flexibility to support margins/RoA in FY27

Bernstein on HDFC BK
O-P, TP Rs 1150
There was no dramatic improvement, but neither was there much to complain about, especially given b/s adjustment in progress.
While growth trends were largely known - slightly weaker loan growth & still healthy deposit growth driving an improvement in LDR - margins showed sequential resilience despite the decline in LDR
Earnings profile was supported by a sharp decline in credit costs & continued cost discipline, which helped offset moderation in non-interest income
As a result, bank delivered steady profitability, with RoA holding at 1.9% & EPS growth at 9% YoY (vs. consensus estimates of 6%).

Jefferies on HDFC BK
Buy, TP Rs 1050
Q4 profit of Rs192bn, up 9% YoY, was in line with estimates.
Topline (NII & Fees) was slightly softer than estimates, but was compensated by operating synergies, lower credit costs & higher treasury gains.
Improved deposit growth of 14% aided loan growth of 12% and ~400bps QoQ fall in LDR.
Momentum in deposit growth & NIMs should be key drivers
Tweak estimates & see 15% CAGR in PBT (ex-treasury)

Nomura on HDFC BK
Buy, TP Rs 950
Soft delivery on NIMs; lower credit costs drive PAT beat
Deposit growth keyamid tight liquidity buffers
Asset quality pristine; healthy loan and deposit growth
Leadership continuity and succession planning remain a key monitorable, with tenure of MD & CEO coming up for renewal (in Oct-26), alongside appointment of a new part-time chairman. Expect HDFCB to deliver RoA/RoE of 1.8%/14% over FY27-28F

Investec on HDFC BK
Buy, TP Rs 915
A soft operational quarter in 4Q, with a subdued core PPOP performance (+3% YoY, 3% below est) driven by soft NII (+3% YoY) and fees (+6% YoY).
PAT rose 9% YoY to Rs 192bn, with RoA steady at 1.8% and broadly in line with estimates, aided by lower credit costs of 36 bps.
Loan growth remained steady at 12% YoY (+4% QoQ), while deposits grew strongly at 14% YoY (8.6% QoQ), lowering the CD ratio to 95%, with CASA deposits up 10% QoQ to 34.1%.
Deposit growth was aided by a strong periodend balance build up, as avg deposits/CASA growth was lower at 3.6% / 2.2% QoQ
LCR moderated further to 113.5% in 4Q (vs 116% as of 3Q).

@CNBCTV18News @_RituSingh @latha_venkatesh

items.title: RT Nimesh Shah: CITI on ICICI BK Buy, TP Rs 1720 4Q RoA of 2.4% surpassed CitiE, underpinned by near-zero credit cost (3bps), stable NIMs, and acceler...
items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
CITI on ICICI BK
Buy, TP Rs 1720
4Q RoA of 2.4% surpassed CitiE, underpinned by near-zero credit cost (3bps), stable NIMs, and accelerated loan growth of 15.8% YoY/6.0% QoQ.
Positives
i) Broad-based growth momentum — led by rural (gold loans) and business banking, alongside retail (mortgages/PL/CV/CE) too stepping up; corporate portfolio expanded 3% QoQ.
ii) Elevated corporate recoveries, declining retail unsecured stress contained credit cost.
iii) 5bps IT refund interest lifted NIMs by 2bps.
Offsets:
i) Modest fee growth (7% YoY).
ii) Rs1.06bn treasury loss.
iii) Overhead costs rise (+14% YoY). iv) Deposits growth of 10.8% YoY/3.8% QoQ.
Outlook: Raise loan growth estimates to 15-16%, expect range-bound NIMs, agri-PSL provision reversal offers credit cost buffer.
Earnings estimates broadly retained — credit cost comfort, opex leverage, and improved growth offset by stable NIM and modest non-interest income.

CLSA on ICICI BK
O-P, TP Rs 1700
4QNII & PPOP were largely in line with estimates, while PBT was a 10% above due to negligible credit costs
Latter was driven by sharp recoveries from written-off accounts.
However, even excluding that, core asset quality was better than estimates
Key +ve was pickup in loan growth, up from 12% YoY last quarter to 16% YoY in 4QFY26
This should put to rest worries that ICICIB’s loan growth is slowing.
NIM was largely stable QoQ; a decent outcome in this environment.
Fee income remains sluggish as credit card spending is muted.
Lastly, asset quality continues to surprise positively with the gross/net slippage ratios improving 40bps/10bps YoY.

Nomura on ICICI BK
Buy, TP Rs 1620
4QFY26: Loan growth re-accelerates, credit costs surprise
Normalised PAT beat of 2%
Asset quality pristine; loan growth re-accelerates
With LCR remaining comfortable at 124%, BK is well positioned to defend near-term loan growth and NIMs
FY27-28F EPS are broadly unchanged, as lower credit costs assumptions are offset by softer fee income
Expect BK to deliver sector-leading 2.2% RoA & 16% RoE over FY27-28F.

Jefferies on ICICI Bk
Buy, TP Rs 1670
Q4 profit of Rs137bn, up 8% YoY, was ahead of estimates aided by significantly lower credit costs helped by some corporate recoveries and lower slippages in unsecured loans.
Pickup in loan growth to 16% is encouraging, aided by deposit growth of 11% and headroom in LDR now at 87% (83% last year)
See 13% CAGR in PBT (ex-treasury) over FY26-29E & ROE of 16% in FY27E.
Valuation at 2.2x FY27E adj PB is at deserved premium to peers.

Kotak Inst Eqt on ICICI BK
Buy, TP Rs 1800
Delivered 8% yoy earnings growth in 4QFY26 led by a sharp yoy decline in provisions.
Headline loan growth (16% yoy) and deposit growth (11% yoy) have improved, but operating profit growth was weak (3% yoy).
Asset quality indicators stayed stable.
As a sector leader, ICICI Bank has to deal with elevated expectations that are challenging to meet.
Combined with its valuation premium, this makes outperformance difficult
Like balance sheet resilience over growth.

Investec on ICICI BK
Buy, TP raised to Rs 1625
A strong Q4FY26, with PAT up 8% YoY and 21% QoQ to Rs 137bn (+6% ahead of est) with best-in-class RoA delivery of 2.4%.
Importantly, ICICI delivered strong NII growth (+8% YoY and 4.8% QoQ) with a sharp pickup in loan growth (+16% YoY vs 11.5% YoY as of 3Q) and stable NIMs (at 4.32%).
Loan growth was strong and broadbased at 16% YoY, led by business banking (+24% YoY) and rural loans (+26% YoY).
Core fee growth was however soft at 6% YoY, and higher opex capped core PPoP growth at 5% YoY.
Asset quality remained robust, with net slippages at just 35bp (annualized), and credit cost at just 3bp (vs 37bp in 3Q), helping drive 30bp QoQ RoA expansion

MS on ICICI Bk
OW TP Rs 1705
PAT beat MSe by 7%, largely driven by lower provisions.
Core PPOP was 1% above MSe, reflecting higher NII.
Adjusted NIM dipped 2bp QoQ to 4.27% (MSe 4.25%).
Loan growth accelerated to 16% vs. 10% in 2QF26.
Resilience with growth amid geopolitical uncertainties -

Bernstein on ICICI BK
O-P, TP Rs 1550
Q4 addressed key concern—loan growth—with bank delivering 16% YoY growth after several quarters of trailing system
However, deposit growth remained modest, particularly relative to system, possibly reflecting a conscious effort to protect margins
Overall, PPoP growth was somewhat subdued, weighed down by weak noninterest income & steady cost growth
A sharp decline in credit costs, however, provided a meaningful offset, supporting earnings growth.
As a result, profitability improved, with RoA expanding to 2.4% despite modest underlying operating trends.

JPM on ICICI BK
OW. TP Rs 1600
Delivered another quarter of industry leading growth with net advances up by 6% q/q, which came in better vs JPMe at 4.6% and vs large private banks at ~4% q/q
Thesis of positive inflection in NII growth was visible in 4Q as well with NII rising by 8.4% y/y vs 7.7%/7.4% over the last 2 quarters.
With probability of further rate cuts reducing significantly, expect NII growth to accelerate further to 12.3%/15.1% in FY28/28
Also, ICICIB affirmed no concerns in its loan portfolio (especially Business Banking which is 21% of advances) from the ongoing supply chain disruptions from the West Asia conflict, which is comforting

UBS on ICICI BK
Buy, TP Rs 1720
Q4 PAT beat driven by negligible provisions; Margins expand 2bp QoQ
Loan growth at c6% QoQ; LDR down 170bp
Bank confident on loan growth while NIMs to be range-bound
Est RoA of c2.2% (FY27-29E) for ICICI & expect most metrics to remain steady
STK trading at c2.x March 2027 PBV
@CNBCTV18News @_RituSingh @latha_venkatesh

items.title: RT Sridhar Vembu: I am happy to see the YCombinator wave arriving in India. Our co-founder Tony and I were in silicon valley during the original YC wa...
items.description_html:
RT Sridhar Vembu
I am happy to see the YCombinator wave arriving in India. Our co-founder Tony and I were in silicon valley during the original YC wave of companies. We learned a lot from the YC companies. We also made the conscious choice to pursue a different course.

In the ancient Bharatiya tradition of philosophical debate, I am going to offer this.

I will start with things I agree with YC on.

1. The biggest lesson anyone can learn from YC: small passionate teams can do magic. This was true way before AI coding arrived and will always be true.

2. YC is absolutely right to not over-emphasize "innovation". Doing a similar product as the bigger guys, faster and cheaper, is often the best course. Google did not invent search. OpenAI did not invent the LLM. Anthropic did not invent agentic coding.

3. YC companies tend to geographically cluster, and that can lead to subtle peer pressure and group-think. So the "laggard" founders who are not growing like a weed every week start to feel left out and eventually the result is "founder depression". And YC has counselors.

By the standards of 2007 silicon valley or even 2014 silicon valley, we were thought to be losers. Just keep that in mind.

4. Many deep tech problems like building a better, cheaper MRI machine or advanced semiconductor equipment require long focus and patient execution and lots of capital. These are endurance tests, not weekly sprints.

5. YC too often optimizes companies for "exit". That philosophy was built for and requires prolonged bubbles, which American policy has delivered, at the price of nearly wrecking the country (note the extreme inequality and political division). If you love India, you should not wish for similar bubbles.

6. YC model worked in silicon valley. One of the reasons it worked was that silicon valley could get any talent from anywhere in the world, notably from India, easily. That era may have ended or at least on pause right now.

Bengaluru has tried the same thing but with "any talent from anywhere in India" and we have not yet created huge companies. India needs its Huawei and Xiaomi and BYD and these companies are Chinese to the core, built by patriotic Chinese. Indian talent, staying in India, rooted in India, is going to have to build companies like them. Enough said.

items.title: RT Captain Brijesh Chowta ಕ್ಯಾಪ್ಟನ್ ಬ್ರಿಜೇಶ್ ಚೌಟ: Long before modern shipyards, the rivers and coasts of Tulunadu built vessels th...
items.description_html:
RT Captain Brijesh Chowta ಕ್ಯಾಪ್ಟನ್ ಬ್ರಿಜೇಶ್ ಚೌಟ
Long before modern shipyards, the rivers and coasts of Tulunadu built vessels that sailed across the Arabian Sea. Over time, that legacy faded into memory. Today, with the revival of shipbuilding in Mangalore, that relationship with the sea is finding form once again.

Wonderful to see a once-defunct maritime facility brought back to life - now transformed into a modern shipbuilding hub by Chowgule Shipyard and delivering its first vessel, FRISIAN FUTURE.

Built in Mangalore for global trade, this 8,500 DWT export vessel reflects not just engineering capability, but the revival of local industry, jobs, and confidence along our coast.

With Hon’ble PM Shri Narendra Modi’s vision of strengthening India’s maritime sector, Mangalore is steadily finding its place on the shipbuilding map of India with global orders.

An open invitation to #Shipbuilding players - come build from #Mangalore.

As for Mangaloreans in Shipbuilding, inviting you to Come #BackToOoru and rebuild the legacy here, once again.

items.title: Imagine a Sector where Companies: 1⃣Operate in a regulated oligopoly with very high entry barriers 2⃣Cash Rich B/S with Healthy Dividend payout rati...
items.description_html:
Imagine a Sector where Companies:

1⃣Operate in a regulated oligopoly with very high entry barriers
2⃣Cash Rich B/S with Healthy Dividend payout ratio of 50%+
3⃣20%+ Return on capital employed
4⃣Convert 100% of Operating profit into OCF (FCF machines)

Sounds like a dream right?

That is exactly the structure of Credit Rating Agencies

Three key listed players in this space:
1⃣CRISIL - subsidiary of S&P Global
2⃣ICRA - subsidiary of Moody's
3⃣CARE Ratings - Indian promoter led independent rating agency
4⃣India Ratings - subsidiary of Fitch Ratings (unlisted)

Rating agencies are capital light financial infrastructure businesses. Their core role is to assess credit risk of companies, banks, NBFCs, bonds, structured products & government backed instruments.

They sit at the center of the debt market ecosystem.

Whenever companies raise debt, refinance loans, issue bonds, securitize pools or structure instruments – Ratings are required.

This is a regulated oligopoly with global parentage, strong credibility backing, and very high reputation led entry barriers. Once credibility is established, the business becomes sticky with strong operating leverage.

Their Primary REV streams include
- Credit rating of corporate debt instruments, bank loan ratings, structured finance ratings, SME ratings, monitoring fees on outstanding rated instruments, research and analytics services, risk advisory and consulting

Most importantly
Once a rating is assigned, companies continue paying annual surveillance fees
This creates recurring REV visibility.

This makes them indirect beneficiaries of:
- Credit growth, bond market expansion, securitisation activity, structured finance growth & regulatory compliance requirements

This creates a steady stream of issuer paid rating income along with recurring surveillance revenue.
As credit markets deepen, rating activity increases.

What is becoming interesting now across all Rating agencies is the visible acceleration in growth.

With system credit growth moving back to mid teens, bond markets expanding, securitisation activity improving & infrastructure financing picking up - Operating environment for rating agencies is turning favourable again

This is a space worth studying.

items.title: RT Ali Hashem علي هاشم: Most important takeaway from Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf’s interview with State TV, “We made progress in the Islamabad n...
items.description_html:
RT Ali Hashem علي هاشم
Most important takeaway from Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf’s interview with State TV, “We made progress in the Islamabad negotiations, but the gaps are wide and some fundamental points remain.”

MBG covered several topics in what could be described as a message before a turning point, tone was explanatory, less rhetorical, carried both political and strategic messages.

Here are points I found interesting:

📌 It is clear that the enemy was defeated, but this is different from saying that we destroyed their army. When the enemy does not achieve its goals, it means it has been defeated

📌The enemy intended to bring counter-revolutionaries into the country from our western and eastern borders to create chaos inside, but it did not succeed and was defeated.

📌All the enemy's effort was to impose their demands on us, and it is important that we assert our rights; therefore, negotiation is a method of struggle here.

📌Securing the nation's rights must be our main goal, and rest assured that there will be no surrender in the diplomatic arena.

📌I emphasize that we have always sought to normalize transit through the Strait of Hormuz and still do; if it has stopped now, it is because the ceasefire was not fully established in Lebanon.

📌If we were negotiating with the US at the time Trump said so, then why were there so many ultimatums? Why were they threatening to strike our infrastructure?

📌 We did not conduct any negotiations until 48 hours before the ceasefire. Trump is a master of lying and psychological warfare. Yesterday he posted 8 tweets, 7 of which were lies.

📌 Our negotiations began on Saturday when we arrived in Pakistan, and before that, only in the last 48 hours of the war leading to the ceasefire, messages were exchanged through Pakistan.

📌Our approach was that our entry into the 10-point negotiation framework would be when the Lebanon ceasefire was established and Iran's frozen assets were released.

📌Basically, our first meeting with Mr. Asim Munir as a mediator lasted 2 hours and 15 minutes in Islamabad, focusing on the Lebanon ceasefire. His first meeting with the Americans was also on this subject. From there, we saw their efforts to pursue the ceasefire begin, and they implemented the ceasefire incompletely.

📌Currently, we also have disagreements on several issues related to the nuclear matter and the Strait of Hormuz. Of course, the essence of negotiation is for understanding, but we have our principles.

📌The Zionist regime and America have constantly had a cycle of war - ceasefire - peace, and this cycle must be broken, and sustainable peace must be established with guarantees that war will not happen again.

📌We have reached conclusions on some issues, but not on others, and other proposals have been made; we are still far from a final discussion.

📌We insist on some issues which are non-negotiable for us, and their number is one or two, and the other side also has similar points.

📌In summary, we made progress in the Islamabad negotiations, but the gaps are wide and some fundamental points remain.

📌We have tried to follow the experiences of past periods and the distrust towards the U.S., and (in case of agreement) our policy will also be step-by-step action; it should not be that we fulfill our commitments but they do not; in case of agreement, we take one step and they must also take one step.

items.title: Raamdeo Agrawal Sir on how his investing journey started – "During my CA course in Mumbai, I somehow got hooked onto the stock markets. I got to lear...
items.description_html:
Raamdeo Agrawal Sir on how his investing journey started –

"During my CA course in Mumbai, I somehow got hooked onto the stock markets. I got to learn about companies like Century Textiles, where I was doing the audits. Meanwhile, in 1980, my brother got into the merchant navy. He was making good money - around Rs.30,000-40,000 a month. He had saved about Rs. 4-5 lakh.

I suggested he invest that money in the stock market. I told him that I would help him invest. So that’s how I started my journey of investing in the stock markets in 1980, with my brother’s money. We went to the brokers for information, started reading accountancy books (which were not readily available), and international magazines like Fortune, which carried stock-oriented articles.

The good thing was that I was a habitual reader right from my childhood. As far as I can remember, I was a voracious reader. I used to go to the library almost every day and borrow whichever books I could lay my hands on, though it was mostly fiction.

After I completed my CA, I wondered what to do in life. The only thing that interested me was the stock market. I tried to practice as a CA for a year but realised pretty early that it was not my cup of tea. Then I decided to get into equity research. I

saw an advertisement for an equity research job in a company called ‘Aditi Investments’. It was like a God-sent offer for me. Can you believe that I was the only person who applied for that job? I got the job.

By that time, I had collected and read balance sheets for about 250 companies. My weekend job and passion were to collect balance sheets. I was so fascinated by everything. In the early 1980s, there were no databases or the internet. If I met somebody and got to know that his father invests in the stock market, I would make it a point to go to his house, make friends with his father, and gather all the balance sheets!

So, by 1985, I had read and internalised all the balance sheets by page. I had analysed them by capacity, location, utilization, cost structure, profit structure, etc. I became a moving encyclopaedia on that many stocks. Simultaneously, I was investing Rs. 5-6 lakh for my brother. I used to think I was a smart person. I did two years of formal equity research. I wrote a book, Corporate Numbers Game, in 1986

From the Book Masterclass with Super Investors

items.title: RT Danny (Dennis) Citrinowicz ,داني سيترينوفيتش: I don’t know what has been presented in the White House, but this is the adversary st...
items.description_html:
RT Danny (Dennis) Citrinowicz ,داني سيترينوفيتش
I don’t know what has been presented in the White House, but this is the adversary status I would present, with one important
Takeaway - At this stage, the most important requirement for U.S. policy is strategic realism about what the use of force can still achieve against Iran.

1. Iran’s leadership is not interested in prolonging the war, but it believes it holds an advantage in a “pain tolerance” competition vis-à-vis the United States and the international community.

2. Threats of force are unlikely to produce meaningful concessions in negotiations. Iran is expected to hold firmly to the red lines it has maintained from the outset.

3. Maritime pressure will not compel Iranian capitulation. Absent an agreement, it is more likely to lead to direct confrontation between the U.S. and Iran. Interdictions of Iranian shipping may accelerate escalation, but not surrender.

4. The current Iranian leadership will not give up uranium enrichment, its missile program, support for its regional network of proxies, or its strategic influence over the Strait of Hormuz.

5. Limited strikes will not alter this trajectory. Tehran is not easily swayed by signaling. A broader campaign against Iran’s civilian infrastructure could severely damage its economy, but would almost certainly trigger wide-scale retaliation, particularly against energy infrastructure across the Gulf.

6. Without regime change, which would require a dramatic U.S. investment in time, resources, and political will, these core dynamics are unlikely to shift.

7. Decision-making in Iran is becoming more fragmented and hardline, increasingly shaped by the IRGC. This makes coherent compromise more difficult than in the past.

8. What Iran did not concede before or during the conflict, it is even less likely to concede now, regardless of military pressure or blockade measures.

9. In the absence of a deal, escalation is not a risk, it is the default trajectory. Iran is already preparing for it, including efforts to rebuild and reinforce its missile capabilities.

10. To date, even successful operational achievements have not meaningfully changed Iran’s strategic calculus. Paradoxically, the campaign has weakened Iran economically, but strengthened the regime internally, especially among the regime supporters.

+1

The administration seems to have two main options: a framework agreement broadly resembling the previous nuclear deal, Or a wider escalation would impose massive economic costs on Iran, but rather than restrain it, it could drive a significant expansion of its escalatory actions.

#IranWar‌

With ~90% production line validations done with real data + AI simulators that use physics aware models, the efficiency gains will be massive. What's unfolding at Pepsico(with Siemens & NVIDIA) is going to set the narrative for the foreseeable future. Investory management, product mix, precise assortment planning, procurement & sales, pricing mix, all are set to transform. And, this play has many proxy plays that few are willing to look at.

items.title: Strait of Hormuz is open during ceasefire, Iran says. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said the Strait of Hormuz was open following a ceasefire...
items.description_html:
Strait of Hormuz is open during ceasefire, Iran says.
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said the Strait of Hormuz was open following a ceasefire accord agreed in Lebanon, while US President Donald Trump said he believed a deal to end the Iran war would come "soon," although the timing remains unclear.
Araghchi said in a post on X the Strait was open for all commercial vessels for the remainder of the U.S.-brokered 10-day truce between Israeli forces and Iran-backed Hezbollah agreed between Israel and Lebanon.
He said the passage of ships would need to be along the route that Iran's Ports and Maritime Organisation had announced.
@Reuters

items.title: Good way of looking at opportunity size, Case study by Ramesh Damani Sir - "It comes from an understanding of what you are investing in, rather than i...
items.description_html:
Good way of looking at opportunity size, Case study by Ramesh Damani Sir -

"It comes from an understanding of what you are investing in, rather than investing in the price action. That is the difference between fundamentalists and price action-oriented investors. An investor who is price action-oriented will think that the stock has tripled, so let me take some money home.

While the fundamentalist will understand that he has bought something that has deep value and even if the stock has tripled, it doesn’t mean that the value has been extracted.

Let me give you an example from my life to illustrate the point. In 2003-04, when I was trying to look for new themes, I realized that alcoholic beverage stocks were very cheap. You could have bought 50% of India’s liquor industry for a few hundred crores.

It’s staggering to think that - in a country with a 1 + billion population, with no religious inhibition about drinking, and where people are young and getting into drinking age. With Rs. 100 crore, you could have bought 10-20% in the Indian liquor industry or 2 flats in NCPA in Mumbai - that was the value proposition.

You could have seen what Anheuser-Busch is valued at in America and what San Miguel is valued at in the Philippines. Then if you bought with conviction, you would not sell even if it became Rs. 400 crore or Rs. 1000 crore, because that’s silly. It could be valued at Rs. 50,000 crore or Rs. 100,000 crore, or maybe there is no terminal value.

A true fundamentalist will figure that equation out, without knowing the exact timeframes. These just become a core part of the portfolio. That’s the difference between a true value investor, a fundamentalist, and a price action person.

That is where it is important to have a grounding in what you buy. I hung around with a group of people who were very deep-value investors, and that helped me immeasurably in life, because I have seen how they have done it. I have seen with my own eyes how Gillette shares were bought at Rs. 45. The share price went up to Rs. 100 and then Rs. 500, and not even 100 shares were sold, still sitting tight with it. It helps to have a good network."

From the Book Masterclass with Super Investors; no stock reco

items.title: Did you know why Nuclear energy is making a comeback? One kilogram of enriched uranium (4% U-235) produces roughly 160,000 kWh of electricity. For com...
items.description_html:
Did you know why Nuclear energy is making a comeback?

One kilogram of enriched uranium (4% U-235) produces roughly 160,000 kWh of electricity. For comparison:

1 kg of coal → 3 kWh
1 kg of natural gas → 15 kWh
1 kg of diesel → 12 kWh

This is not a marginal improvement. It is a 50,000x advantage over coal by mass. It's why one standard 700 MWe PHWR in India replaces a 2,800 MW coal plant equivalent on annual energy.

items.title: ASML spent 20 years and billions of dollars on tech that "would never work." The problem? EUV light is so extreme that: • Air absorbs it • Glass len...
items.description_html:
ASML spent 20 years and billions of dollars on tech that "would never work."

The problem? EUV light is so extreme that:
• Air absorbs it
• Glass lenses can't handle it
• A single dust particle ruins the process

Every optical system had to be reinvented from scratch.

Competitors gave up. ASML didn't.

By 2018, no chip on earth could be made without their machine.

The lesson: the harder the engineering problem, the wider the moat.

items.title: While most Indian households now own a mobile phone, computer ownership in India has grown slowly. Over the last two decades, the share of households ...
items.description_html:
While most Indian households now own a mobile phone, computer ownership in India has grown slowly. Over the last two decades, the share of households that own a computer has increased from just over 1% to just over 9%. In most developed countries, by contrast, computer ownership exceeds 75%.

Among the 30 million households in India that own a computer, 90% have a laptop, while 20% own a desktop. A little under 10% of computer-owning households report having both a laptop and a desktop.

India has also made limited progress in the ability to use computers over the last decade. A little over one in five adults, or 200 million people, were able to operate a computer as of 2025. This share is roughly twice the share of households that own a computer, suggesting that many people may have learned to use a computer, or use one in college or at work, without owning one themselves.

While the proportion of rural adults who can use a computer has increased, it still stands at just about one in six. Among urban adults, the figure is much higher with over one in three adults, but it has grown only marginally over the last decade.

How does the ability to use computers vary by age group and gender in India? Read @akwaghmare’s piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/computers/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Computer #Technology #Laptop #India #DataForIndia

items.title: RT menaka doshi: In all the politics over Lok Sabha seats an important point deserves attention. India's constitution links delimitation to population...
items.description_html:
RT menaka doshi
In all the politics over Lok Sabha seats an important point deserves attention.

India's constitution links delimitation to population.
But does that create the right development incentives for states?

"A formulaic, population-based approach in one of the most unequal, federal democracies in the world is a flawed approach. Beyond the immediate inequities, such seat distribution would not offer states the right incentives and rewards for development."

- Praveen Chakravarty, Congress Party @pravchak

"There is a tension between the democratic impulse that the vote of every citizen should count equally and the federal impulse that all states should have equal stakes in the national polity.

To use a phrase from economics, there is no corner solution. States and Center will have to find a modus vivendi involving seat adjustments in Lok Sabha and strengthening of other institutions envisaged by the Constitution for protecting state interests such as Rajya Sabha."

- Avinash M Tripathi, Center for Study of Indian Economy, Azim Premji University
@qfint

"Sticking to the original constitutional position of one person, one vote, one value, will allow the northern states to dominate the Indian polity and leave the south feeling powerless. An imperfect union can survive but an unjust union won’t."

- Sanjay Hegde, Senior Advocate
@sanjayuvacha

Read 👇
https://www.bloomberg.com/news/newsletters/2026-04-16/india-s-modi-uses-women-s-quota-to-remake-parliament-with-delimitation-push

items.title: In 2006, while his Matrix Laboratories colleagues toasted their $736M acquisition(one of the largest pharma deals in Indian history), Satyanarayana Ch...
items.description_html:
In 2006, while his Matrix Laboratories colleagues toasted their $736M acquisition(one of the largest pharma deals in Indian history), Satyanarayana Chava was sketching molecules on a napkin.

He quit Matrix Laboratories as their CTO and started Laurus Labs with personal savings.

No VC. No corporate backing.

The Indian pharma industry was a fast-follower game — copy patents, race to market.

He wanted to build something that actually solved problems.

The timing? Brutal. Chinese manufacturers were dumping APIs at prices below raw material cost.

He did it anyway. Laurus Labs is worth ₹61,000 Cr today. The best founders don't wait for the right moment. They create it.

no reco

items.title: Paytm owner One97 Communications has now become an Indian-owned and controlled company (IOCC). Until now, IOCC status has been significant for online ...
items.description_html:
Paytm owner One97 Communications has now become an Indian-owned and controlled company (IOCC).

Until now, IOCC status has been significant for online retailers, as it allows them to hold inventory.

Eternal-owned Blinkit became one through a QIP, Swiggy is close to getting there, and Nykaa has always had this advantage.

Paytm is among the first major listed fintechs to get IOCC status.

There may not be immediate, direct business benefits, but this is important given the scrutiny Paytm has faced over ownership by China’s Alibaba and Ant Financial, which together held 38% before the IPO.

After the IPO in 2021, foreign investors held 81%, which has now come down to just below 50%.

items.title: Updates on GLP 1 market in India CareEdge Ratings estimates that India’s potential GLP‑1 market is set to expand nearly fivefold, from Rs 1,000–Rs ...
items.description_html:
Updates on GLP 1 market in India

CareEdge Ratings estimates that India’s potential GLP‑1 market is set to expand nearly fivefold, from Rs 1,000–Rs 1,200 crore in 2025 to approximately Rs 4,500–5,000 crore by 2030.

Post patent expiry, drug prices are expected to decline by around 40–50% in FY27, followed by a further reduction of 10–30% in FY28 due to heightened competition.

Within 10 days of launch, generic semaglutide has added 40k new patients & already taken 24% value share of India’s sema mkt, with overall vols up 2.5X.

Eli Lilly's Mounjaro (tirzepatide), a competitor to semaglutide, became India's highest-selling drug by value and has overtaken Augmentin by GSK.

items.title: #LaurusLabs, at today’s not-so-cheap valuations and hovering near its all-time highs, is not a HOLD if your view is just 3 months. But stretch that v...
items.description_html:
#LaurusLabs, at today’s not-so-cheap valuations and hovering near its all-time highs, is not a HOLD if your view is just 3 months. But stretch that view to 3 years, and it still might be.

This gap comes down to time. As Howard Marks and Nassim Taleb remind us, risk and value shift with the clock. What feels expensive and fragile in the short run can turn reasonable once time allows the business to prove itself.

In 'Fooled by Randomness', Taleb tells a story about a retired dentist who is a skilled investor. The dentist expects to earn about 15% more than safe government bonds each year. But he checks his portfolio many times a day. This habit causes stress because, in the short term, prices move almost randomly. Taleb calls this “noise”, which simply means meaningless fluctuations that do not reflect real value.

Over very short periods, like minutes or days, the chance of seeing a gain is barely above 50%. This means that over three months, price movements are driven mostly by luck and maybe Q4 FY26 numbers or, rather, the interpretation of those. If a stock is already “not-so-cheap”, its price reflects a lot of optimism. There is little room for disappointment. Even a small negative surprise can push the price down. It's always albout the rate of change relative to the asking run-rate (valuations)

In 'Most Important Thing', Marks explains that risk often comes from paying too high a price. When you buy an expensive stock, you have almost no “margin for error”. This simply means you have little room to be wrong and still be okay. In the short term, bad luck or shifting sentiment can cause sharp falls. He also points out that being early can feel the same as being wrong, especially over short periods.

Taleb offers another example in 'Fooled by Randomness'. He describes the idea of building a ski resort in Miami. Imagine a rare blizzard hits, and the resort makes a huge profit in its first year. That success does not make it a good decision. It was simply luck. In the same way, making money on an expensive stock over 3 months may just mean you were lucky, not right.

Things look different over longer periods. As time passes, real business performance begins to matter more than daily price swings. Taleb notes that over a year, the odds of seeing gains improve greatly. Over 3 years, the company’s actual progress has a much better chance to show through the noise. What the business earns and builds starts to matter more than short-term sentiment.

Back in 2023, I was one of the few bullish voices on Laurus. Talk is cheap, so I backed that view with my own money. Sell ratings were common at the time, and many were anchored to targets around 250, which weighed heavily on sentiment.

Marks explains that while prices can drift in the short run, terminal value pulls them back over time. Terminal value is simply what a business is truly worth based on what it can earn and build.

For Laurus Labs, the CDMO business is that anchor. Over the next five to seven years, this segment alone could cross 10,000 Cr. in annual sales. Then, post-2033, the precision fermentation and bio-engine should be all guns blazing.

If a company grows well, it can “grow into” a high MCap. This is why a stock that looks expensive today may still work out over a few years. Over 3 years, a strong business may justify a high starting price. Over 3 months, you are mostly exposed to chance.

Taleb compares short-term risk to a version of Russian roulette with many chambers. You might avoid losses several times, but the risk is always there. Without a margin of safety, a sudden drop can happen at any time. This is why short-term investing in expensive stocks can feel unpredictable and fragile.

In the end, time changes the nature of the bet. Over 3 months, randomness dominates, and a high price leaves little protection. Over 3 years, business performance has time to come through, and value can catch up with price. The same stock can look risky or reasonable depending on how long you plan to hold it.

Laurus Labs is our core family holding, and none of this is an investment recommendation.
